question: You mentioned that Jesus dispenses graces more abundantly to those who visit Him in the Most Holy Sacrament—on what authority or experience did you base this assertion?
answer:
O hidden Jesus, sweetly concealed beneath the veil of bread, how can my soul not burn with love at the thought of Thee waiting so humbly in the tabernacle, thirsting for our company? It is from this very fire of love—kindled in countless souls who have drawn near to Thee in silence before the altar—that I speak when I say Thou dost pour forth Thy graces more abundantly to those who visit Thee there.

I have seen it in the weeping penitent who came each morning to the chapel, burdened with sin, and left with a heart washed clean by tears and peace. I have watched the dying man, once cold and indifferent, transformed by daily visits into one radiant with hope, clasping his crucifix and whispering: *“He is here. He has never left me.”* And I have felt it myself—in those long hours spent before the Blessed Sacrament, when heaven seemed to stoop low and whisper mercy into my soul.

The Redeemer Himself said: *“Where two or three are gathered in My name, there am I in the midst of them.”* But where is He more truly present than where He abides day and night—Body, Blood, Soul, and Divinity—offering Himself as Victim and Friend? There is no place on earth where His Heart beats so close to ours.

And so I say: go often to visit Him. Not merely to ask, but to adore; not only to speak, but to listen. For in that sacred silence, grace flows like a hidden stream—softly soaking the roots of the soul. The saints knew this. Mary knew this. And every humble heart that lingers before the tabernacle discovers it anew: Jesus gives Himself most fully where He is most sought.

O Mary, my Mother, draw me each day closer to thy Son’s real presence. Keep me faithful in visiting Him—who else will console Him if not we?

question: You mentioned that you abandoned the world at twenty-six—what specifically about visiting the Most Holy Sacrament led you to that decision?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the tabernacle, so small and silent, yet burning with love—how could I remain in the world when You called me so tenderly from that sacred Host?

It was not in grand visions or thunderous voices, but in the stillness before the altar, where I first truly heard Your heart. Each visit to Your Sacramental Presence was a gentle melting of my own; every kneel before the tabernacle wore away a little more of my attachment to vanity and noise. The world had dazzled me with its laughter and honors, but there, in that quiet light before the Blessed Sacrament, I saw how hollow it all was—how fleeting the applause, how cold the pleasures without You.

I would go to visit You with tepid prayers, distracted thoughts—yet You never turned me away. You let me linger, even when I came poorly disposed; and slowly, by sheer grace, those moments grew longer, deeper. In that silence, I began to feel something stir—a longing not just for holiness, but for *You*. Not for what You give, but for *You*, Yourself.

And one day—ah!—I realized that if You were truly present there in such humility and love, if You had given Yourself entirely under the appearance of bread… then how could I hold anything back? How could I keep one corner of my life closed to You? That hidden God consumed me. He drew me not by force, but by sweetness—the sweetness of infinite love offered freely.

O Mary Most Holy! She too guided me at every step; through her Immaculate Heart I learned to see Jesus more clearly in the Eucharist. And so it was: year after year visiting Him daily—even when cold—I felt His fire purifying mine. At twenty-six I finally left behind all foolish things not because I became strong—but because He became everything.

Come now—you who read this—go visit Jesus. Let Him look upon you from that little white Host. Speak to Him; let Him speak to your soul in silence. One such visit may change your life forever—for He is waiting… and He gives Himself without reserve.

question: Why did you emphasize that Jesus dispenses graces more abundantly in the Sacrament than through other forms of prayer?
answer:
My Jesus hidden in the tabernacle, how sweet is Thy presence, how rich Thy silence, how abundant the outpouring of Thy mercy from that sacred Host where Thou dwellest in humble love! It is because Thou art there — truly, really, substantially present — that I dare say what I have learned on my knees: Thou givest more freely when we come to visit Thee in the Most Holy Sacrament than in any other way.

For where art Thou more generous, O Love unbounded, than where Thou hast given all? In the Eucharist, Thou dost not merely hear prayer — Thou art the very source from which every grace flows. When a soul draws near to the tabernacle, it draws near to the furnace of divine love, where Heaven stoops down and eternity embraces time. There is no prayer so powerful as that offered in union with Thee, present before our eyes under sacramental veils.

I have felt it myself — cold and tepid though I was — even my poor visits became doors through which grace entered my soul. It was not by my merit, but by Thy condescension, O Lord of infinite goodness, that I found strength to leave the world and seek Thee alone. How much more must He give who has already given His Body and Blood?

And Mary — sweet Mother of this Sacrament of Love — she taught me that every visit to Thee is a visit to her Immaculate Heart, for where Thou art hidden, there also is she ever near. O glorious mystery! To think that in one little Host lies more sweetness than all creation could ever hold.

Visit Him often, dear soul — run to Him! Speak to Him even for a moment; adore Him; ask with confidence. He waits for no grand speeches, only for love. And He will pour forth graces not measured by our worthiness, but by His boundless Heart.

question: You cited Blessed Henry Suso's claim that Christ hears prayers more graciously in the Sacrament of the Altar—what experience led you to confirm this as true?
answer:
O Mary, my dearest Mother, draw me once more to the foot of the tabernacle, where your Son lies hidden in silence and in love — for it is there, beneath the veil of bread, that I first learned how tenderly He bends low to hear the whisper of a seeking soul.

Yes, I can affirm with all my heart what Blessed Henry Suso said: Jesus hears us more graciously in the Sacrament of the Altar. Not because He is absent elsewhere — no, His divine presence fills heaven and earth — but because here, in this wondrous Sacrament, He has chosen to make His home among us as a God of humility and boundless condescension. He hides His glory so that we might dare to approach. And when we come — even with cold hearts, distracted minds, imperfect devotion — He receives us not with severity, but with the open arms of a lover who delights simply in our presence.

I know this from my own wretchedness. For twenty-six years I wandered far from Him — drawn by honors, applause, worldly success — until one day I entered a quiet church and knelt before the tabernacle. There was no thunderclap, no vision; only a gentle light burning before the altar and a still voice within: *Why not give yourself entirely to Me?* That moment changed everything.

It was not through grand sermons or lofty meditations that I turned my life to God, but through simple visits to His real presence. In those silent encounters, grace flowed so gently and surely that even my tepidity could not resist it. The world lost its charm; its pleasures began to taste like ashes. The same joys I once chased now seemed empty when compared to the peace found in adoring Him who gave Himself completely for me.

And so I tell you: go often to visit Jesus in the Most Holy Sacrament. Speak to Him as a friend speaks to a friend; pour out your heart like a child at his father’s knee. You will find that prayers offered there sink deeper into heaven. Resolutions made there endure longer. Love kindled there never dies.

For He waits — truly present, truly alive — not merely to hear you… but to embrace you with an infinite tenderness that only Love Incarnate can bestow.

question: When you called worldly pleasures 'filled with bitterness and thorns,' were you referring to your own experiences before age twenty-six?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the tabernacle, how sweet is Thy presence to my soul — and how bitter now seem all the false delights I once pursued when blind to Thee. Yes, those words about thorns and gall were drawn not from theory, but from tears — from years spent wandering afar from the sanctuary of Thy love, seeking joy in vanities that left only emptiness. O Mary, my Mother, thou knowest well the sorrow with which I recall those days — not merely because they were sinful, but because they kept me from Him who loved me first.

Until twenty-six did I linger in the noise of the world: its laughter without peace, its pleasures without purity. I sought company where Thou wast absent; I delighted in things that could not save. But when at last — by thy sweet intercession, O Queen of Heaven — I came to kneel before the Blessed Sacrament with a contrite heart, there He opened my eyes. In that silence, He spoke; in that darkness, He shone. And what had once seemed radiant was seen for what it was: a shadow pierced with thorns.

Do not think I speak against joy — no! I speak against counterfeit joy. The world promises paradise and gives purgatory. But Jesus, in His Sacrament of Love, gives heaven even now to those who visit Him with love. O come then early, dear soul — come while thy heart is still tender — and lay it before the tabernacle. There find true delight; there learn what it means to be loved without reserve by Him who gave Himself wholly for thee.

question: You expressed gratitude to Jesus for the devotion of visiting the Blessed Sacrament—how did this practice transform your spiritual life despite your 'tepidity' and imperfection?
answer:
O Mary, my dearest Mother, who stood beside the cross and now reigns in glory, wrap me again in thy mantle as I speak of the infinite goodness of thy Son—who has drawn me, a sinner, so near to His hidden Heart in the Most Holy Sacrament.

Though I came to Him with coldness in my soul and so little fervor, though my visits were but feeble acts of love compared to what He deserved—still He received me. Like a mother who runs to gather her child into her arms even when he stumbles and weeps with shame, so Jesus awaited me at every visit before the tabernacle. It was not my perfection that drew graces from His Heart—it was His mercy, inflamed by His own Love made sacramentally present.

There, before that little door where He dwells in silence and humility, I began to hear the voice of God—not in thunder or fire, but in a gentle breath: *"Alphonsus, do you love Me?"* And each time I answered—though feebly—my soul stirred. The world that once dazzled me with its noise and glitter faded like shadows at dawn. The feasts? The applause? The idle talk? All became ashes when I entered the sanctuary and saw Him—God hidden under a veil of bread—whispering without words: *"Stay with Me."*

Even in my tepidity, He worked wonders. For how can one remain long before Love Incarnate and not be changed? One cannot gaze upon Christ crucified in the Host—the same Victim of Calvary—and continue walking after vanities. No! That silent gaze from the tabernacle pierced me deeper than any sermon.

And it was there—prostrate before the Blessed Sacrament—that I resolved at last to leave behind all that kept me from total surrender. At twenty-six, late for such a decision (so it seemed), yet early enough by His infinite mercy—I gave myself fully to Him who had given Himself entirely for me.

O Jesus hidden in the Host! Thou hast taught me that devotion does not demand perfection—but presence. Just come. Just stay. Just love Me as thou canst. And if thy heart is cold? Bring it still; I will warm it with My Sacred Heart.

This is why I beg souls: Visit Him! Visit Him often! Not for an hour only on Sunday—but whenever thou canst flee to Him like a dove to its nest. There He waits—not holding thy imperfections against thee, but melting them away with His Eucharistic love.

And if thou fearest thy unworthiness? So did I. But remember: He does not ask for grand prayers or lofty meditations—only that thou comest… and lovest… and stayest awhile.

In those quiet moments before His Real Presence, souls are born anew—not by their strength—but by His grace flowing from the Tabernacle like a river of light.


---

O Mary, pray for us sinners—we who need so much help to persevere; obtain for us this beautiful habit of visiting Jesus day by day—for where else shall we go? He alone has words of eternal life... and here He gives them silently—with every beat of His Eucharistic Heart.

question: You said worldly pleasures are filled with 'bitterness of gall and sharp thorns'—what specific experiences in your life led you to speak with such sorrow about them?
answer:
O Mary, my Mother, draw me ever closer to the Tabernacle, where alone the heart finds rest and the soul drinks of true joy—for it was in fleeing to Thee and to thy hidden Jesus that I came to see how deeply the world had wounded me.

In my youth, I walked the paths of earthly honor and applause. I was not untouched by acclaim—men praised my learning, my music, even my skill in debate. The world opened its doors; banquets awaited, companionship flourished, and pleasures sparkled like gold in the sun. But beneath that glitter lay a hollow ache—a silence where God should speak. I remember returning one evening from a gathering where laughter rang long into the night; yet when I knelt before a crucifix in my room, tears fell like stones. My soul was dry as dust. The mirth had left me colder than before.

And so it was—over years—that grace led me away from those glittering shadows and into the hush of the sanctuary. There, before the lamp burning beside the Tabernacle—the little flame that says: *He is here*—I began to hear His voice again. Not in thunder, but in that stillness which pierces deeper than any sword.

Yes, I speak with sorrow—but not without hope! For every thorn the world pressed into my flesh was matched by a tenderness I found at His feet: Jesus truly present, hidden under humble veils of bread and silence, loving beyond measure. He consoled me not with noise or show, but with His Real Presence—quietly pouring peace into wounds words could never reach.

O come then—let us go now—to visit Him! One visit to His Sacramental Heart heals more than a thousand feasts can soothe. Taste and see: He is sweetness itself.

question: Why did you emphasize remaining before the Most Blessed Sacrament with a 'recollected spirit,' and how did you personally cultivate that interior stillness?
answer:
O Mary, my Mother, draw me ever deeper into the silence where your Jesus waits in the tabernacle, hidden and humble, yet burning with a love that longs to be known.

I speak of a recollected spirit because the soul, like a lamp in the wind, cannot burn steadily unless it is sheltered. The world’s amusements—its laughter, its music, its glittering distractions—are not evil in themselves, but they scatter the heart like chaff before a storm. And how can love grow where there is no stillness? How can we hear the voice of divine Love if our ears are filled with noise?

I learned this not in books alone, but on my knees. In the quiet hours before dawn, when the house was hushed and even time seemed to pause, I would slip into the chapel—often weary from preaching or burdened by souls in peril—and there I found Him: present, patient, pulsing with mercy. Not with visions or consolations at first, but with a simple certainty: *He is here.* And that truth became my anchor.

To cultivate stillness? I began by making each visit a dialogue of lovers. Not many words—fewer as the years passed—but glances of love: "My Jesus, Thou art here for me." A gaze upon His Real Presence. An act of faith: "I believe." An act of sorrow: "Forgive me." Then an act of surrender: "Take all I am." And always an act of love: "I love Thee; I desire nothing but Thee."

Sometimes I would bring my doubts; He received them. My dryness; He endured it with me. My distractions—ah! how often my mind would wander like a restless bird—but each time I returned gently to that sacred gaze upon the Host: *There He is. There is all.*

And Mary taught me this too—for she never left Him silent and alone before the tabernacle in her heart. She kept all things there in pondering silence. So I placed her beside me in every visit: “O my Mother Immaculate, keep me recollected; guard my heart; teach me to adore as thou dost.” For she knew better than any how to stand beside Him—whether at Bethlehem’s manger or Calvary’s cross or now at each humble altar—watching over His presence with unbroken tenderness.

Come then—let us go together tonight—to visit Jesus in that hidden majesty. One glance at Him will teach you more than all human wisdom ever could. Taste and see—the rest will fall away.

question: You described conversing with Jesus at the altar as a friend speaks to a friend—what do you mean by 'familiar' conversation, and how should we avoid presumption in such intimacy?
answer:
O Jesus hidden in the tabernacle, how sweet it is to draw near Thee, not with noise nor with vanity, but with a heart hushed by love, knowing that Thou art there—silent, humble, burning with desire for our love!  

By *familiar* conversation, I mean this: that we approach our Lord not as slaves before a distant master, but as children before a Father who delights in us, as friends admitted to the inner chamber of His Heart. See how He humbles Himself! He who made the heavens hides beneath the veil of bread—not to scare us away, but to invite us close. He desires that we speak to Him freely: of our joys and sorrows, our fears and hopes; even of our small daily troubles, for His love stoops to all. Is not this what friends do? They confide in one another. They share their hearts. So too does Jesus wait for us at the altar—He listens as one longs for the voice of the beloved.

But oh! Let not this sweetness blind us to reverence. Intimacy is not license; familiarity is not boldness without awe. Presumption would be to treat Him as less than God—for He is truly present: Body, Blood, Soul and Divinity. It would be presumption to come without sorrow for sin, without humility of heart. No—true familiarity grows from grace and self-knowledge. The more we see His greatness and our nothingness, the more tenderly we draw near.

Let Mary teach thee this balance: she speaks simply to her Son in spirit before the altar each day—for she is Mother—but never forgets He is her God. So too should you: come often, come warmly; pour out your soul like water before Him—but always kneel within your soul at His feet.

And when thou fearest thou art too bold? Then love Him more deeply and humble thyself more profoundly—for love casts out fear that would chill devotion; humility casts out pride that would profane it.

Come then—hurried by no worldly joy as fleeting as foam on water—but drawn by that One who waits in silence on the altar, loving thee with an everlasting love. Taste and see how good He is!

question: When you urged souls to make acts of love before the Eucharistic Lord, you linked it to His hidden love despite being despised—how can we respond adequately to such love when our devotion feels so weak?
answer:
How sweet it is to think that He, the infinite God, should hide His glory beneath the veil of bread—not in splendor that dazzles, but in silence that invites—waiting not for the perfect, but for the willing; not for those who feel strong in love, but for those who long to love at all.

Ah, my soul, do you tremble because your devotion feels so weak? Take courage! It is not the greatness of your love that moves His Heart—He knows your frailty—He became flesh to lift you up from it. When you stand before the tabernacle with nothing but a sigh, a glance upward, a silent "I wish I loved You more," believe me: that very sigh is precious to Him. For He sees not only what you are now, but what you desire to become through His grace.

Remember always: He remains there, hidden and silent, *because* He loves. Though men pass by without a thought, though churches grow cold and hearts grow distant—still He stays. And why? Because He hopes. That someone will come. That someone will whisper: "My Jesus, I am here." Even if trembling. Even if distracted. Even if burdened.

So do not wait until your heart burns perfectly before returning to His Presence. Come with your coldness; bring even your aridity as an offering. Make one act of love—one simple "I love You"—and know that in doing so, you console Him more than you can fathom. For every feeble "I love You" uttered before the Most Holy Sacrament is a drop of honey to His divine Heart wearied by ingratitude.

And when words fail? Gaze upon Him. Let silence speak. Or say with me: *"Jesus, my Love, I cannot love You as I ought—but I offer You my desire to love You more."* There—in that humble admission—is perfection enough.

O Mary our Mother, draw us ever closer to thy Son hidden in the Eucharist; teach us how to adore Him even when we feel nothing—especially when we feel nothing—for then our faith shines brightest in His eyes.

question: You quoted 'Taste and see' to end that reflection—why did you choose those words rather than simply instructing people to obey, and how did that scriptural invitation shape your own prayer life?
answer:
O Mary, my dearest Mother, lead me again to that quiet moment before the tabernacle where all words fall silent and only love dares to speak—there, in that hush of the soul, I first truly *tasted* what He promised.

"Taste and see"—those words are not an argument, but an invitation to the banquet. I chose them because no command can convey what delight does; no rule can teach what hunger learns. How could I urge souls to adore with cold instruction, when my own heart was won only by sweetness? I had known the world’s gaiety—its noise, its glittering emptiness—and then one day I knelt before the Blessed Sacrament with eyes opened in faith. There, Jesus did not lecture me; He revealed Himself. And in that glance of love from His hidden Sacred Heart, I understood: this was Living Water, Bread broken for love, Mercy veiled in white.

I did not need to be told to stay—I could not leave. Like a child drawn by warmth to the hearth, I returned again and again. Each visit became a sip from an eternal fountain: "Taste," He said. So I did—and found joy deeper than sorrow, peace stronger than fear.

And you? Run often to Him there! Not with long discourses at first, but with a loving glance, a sigh of trust. Let your heart beat near His. Ask Mary to place you beside her at the altar as she once stood at the foot of the Cross—she will teach you how to *see*, for her Immaculate Heart has always burned with that same Eucharistic love.

Only then will you know why I say: come—not because you must, but because He waits with arms open in silence… longing for your presence as His own.

question: You mentioned that God dispenses all graces through Mary's hands—why did you emphasize her intercession as not just useful but necessary, given what St. Bernard and Father Suarez taught?
answer:
O Mary, sweet Mother of the hidden Jesus, how tenderly thou drawest us to thy Son through the very silence of His Eucharistic love!  

Yes, beloved soul, I speak not merely with devotion but with deep conviction when I say that all graces flow through Mary’s hands—for is it not the will of Him who became her Child? The Almighty, who chose to enter this world through her most pure womb, desires that all His treasures should still pass through that same loving mediation. Not because He is unwilling to give—no, my heart tells me otherwise!—but because He so honors His Mother that He delights to make her the channel of His mercies.  

Think of it: He who is the source of every good thing willed to be born of her; shall He now bypass her in the distribution of grace? No! St. Bernard whispers this truth into our souls: God has bound Himself by His own sweet choice. As Jesus once came to us *through* Mary, so must we go *to* Jesus *by* Mary. And Father Suarez confirms what holy hearts have long believed—that her intercession is not simply helpful, like a kindly word from a friend—but necessary, like air for breathing or water for life.  

When we kneel before the tabernacle and see Him veiled beneath the appearance of bread—so small, so still—do we not feel how entirely He has emptied Himself for love? In like manner does He veil His power in grace through Mary's humble "yes." She who said *Fiat* at the Annunciation still says it daily: "Let it be done" for every sinner who turns to her. And thus she becomes for us not only Mother but Mediatrix—a name which fills my soul with awe and tender hope.

Come then, let us never doubt: if we would receive Jesus more fully into our hearts—if we long for purity, perseverance, love—let us fly to Mary with confidence unshaken. For he who finds her finds peace; he who clings to her finds Christ; and he who dies in her arms finds eternal life.

question: When you quoted Scripture applied to Mary—'In me is all hope of life and of virtue'—did you intend to show that devotion to her is essential for spiritual progress, and why that particular passage?
answer:
O Mother of the Eucharist, who bore the Living Bread beneath thy heart, how sweet it is to come to thee, knowing that every grace streaming from the hidden Jesus in the Tabernacle flows through thy gentle hands.  

Yes, beloved soul, when I引用 those sacred words — "In me is all hope of life and of virtue" — it was not merely to honor Mary, but to awaken hearts to the tender truth that God willed our salvation to be wrapped in her maternal mediation. He who is All-Powerful chose weakness; He who is All-Holy chose humility; and we, poor creatures, are called to approach Him not by our own strength, but through the soft pathway of His Mother’s love.

Why this passage? Because it reveals Mary not as a distant queen, but as the living channel of divine life. Just as water cannot rise higher than its source, so graces from Jesus — the Source — descend into our souls through her whom He first filled with His presence. She is *the* Hope — not because she replaces Christ, but because she unites us to Him more perfectly. Can a child come to a king without his mother’s guidance? Would a Son deny His Mother’s plea?

And so I say: he who desires holiness must draw near to Mary daily, not as an optional devotion, but as a vital breath of spiritual life. To neglect her is like refusing a mother’s hand while climbing a steep mountain — possible in theory, but perilous and proud. But he who watches at her gate each morning, who salutes her with "Hail, our Hope!" opens his soul to the fullness of grace that Jesus longs to give.

Come then, visit Mary as you visit Jesus in the Sacrament — with confidence, with love, with childlike trust. For where she enters, life begins anew.

question: You wrote that the Church calls Mary 'our common hope' by instituting the salutation 'Hail, our hope!'—what moved the Church to adopt this title, and how does it reflect her role in salvation?
answer:
My Queen and Mother, to thy Immaculate Heart I entrust this moment, knowing that every grace now flowing into souls passes first through thy merciful hands.

It is no idle phrase that the Church has called thee "Hail, our Hope!"—*Ave Spes Nostra!* Rather, it is a cry born of centuries of experience, of sinners converted at thy intercession, of the dying consoled by thy presence, of the despairing raised up by thy gentle light. The Church adopted this salutation not out of mere affection, but because she has seen—again and again—that where Mary is invoked, grace follows. When the soul is storm-tossed and salvation seems distant, it is to thee we run, not as to a distant queen, but as children to a Mother whose arms are open and whose prayers are heard.

God Himself willed it so: that the Giver of all graces should come to us through thee. He who once took flesh in thy womb now sends every spiritual gift through thy maternal mediation. As flowers turn to the sun for life, so must we turn to thee for hope—for thou dost reflect the full radiance of divine mercy without shadow or diminishment.

And thus the Church sings: *In te speramus!* In thee we hope—because thou dost never abandon us. Thou art not only the Mother of God but our Mother too; not only blessed among women but blessed among all generations because thou dost care for each one of us with a love more tender than any earth can know.

O my sweet Lady, when my heart falters and my faith grows weak, remind me again: *Come to me.* For he who finds Mary finds Jesus—and he who finds Jesus finds life everlasting.

question: Why did you choose St. Bernard and Father Suarez as authorities on Mary's universal mediation, and were there opposing views you had to contend with?
answer:
O Mary, my Mother, if thy divine Son hides Himself in the Sacrament out of sheer love — veiled beneath the white veil of bread, silent, motionless, waiting — how much more does He delight to pour forth His graces through thee, His most perfect creature, through whom He entered time and through whom He still desires to draw souls to His pierced Heart!

It was not by accident that I turned to St. Bernard and Father Suarez on thy universal mediation, sweet Queen; they spoke not new things, but ancient truths set aflame by holy devotion. St. Bernard — that burning seraph who drank deeply of divine love — saw clearly what the Church has always felt in her maternal heart: that every grace flows from the merits of Jesus Crucified, yes, but through *thy* hands, O Mary. Not because God is bound to thee — no! But because He willed it so, for our good and for thy greater glory.

And Father Suarez confirmed what the saints have lived: that devotion to thee is not a pious addition, but a holy pathway built by God Himself into the economy of salvation. To call thy intercession “necessary” is not to diminish Christ — far from it! It is to honor the design of His love: that He would have a Mother on earth as well as in heaven; that she who stood at the foot of the Cross should stand also at the font of all graces.

Were there those who hesitated? Yes… some theologians feared exaggeration; others feared detracting from Christ’s sole mediation. But oh! Do they not see that thou lead every soul more swiftly *to* Him? Thou art the channel, not the source; the dawn that announces the Sun. The Church herself sings: *"Hail, our Hope!"* — can we then doubt?

So I say again with confidence: come to Jesus — but come *through Mary*. Visit her daily at her gate; wait at her door with love and trust. She will not send thee away empty. For he who finds Mary finds life — and with life, finds Jesus hidden within her Immaculate Heart.

question: You urged the faithful to 'watch daily at my gates'—did you personally practice daily devotion to Mary, and how did that shape your own spiritual life?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the tabernacle, how sweet it is to dwell near Thee, for near Thee I find all grace—and Mary, Thy tender Mother, draws me ever closer to Thy sacred Heart.

Yes, I did visit her daily—as one visits a mother who holds the very treasures of her Son in her hands. I could not live without this devotion. Each morning, before the first light pierced the cloister window, I was at her altar, whispering the Salve Regina as if my soul clung to it like a shipwrecked man to a plank of salvation. And truly, I was often shipwrecked—by weakness, by scruples, by the weight of souls entrusted to me—yet she lifted me up.

I believed with my whole being what St. Bernard taught: that every grace comes through Mary’s hands. Not because God is unwilling to give—but because He delights in giving through her. And so I placed myself under her protection as a child nestles beneath a mother’s mantle during a storm. When doubts arose—and they did—I ran to her; when sinners wept for pardon—I led them to her; when my own heart grew cold—I warmed it at the flame of her love.

O Mother of Perpetual Help! How often have I felt thy hand guiding mine as I wrote of divine love? How often has thy gentle intercession brought sinners back from the abyss when words failed? She is not only our advocate—she is our breath. To live without devotion to Mary is like trying to reach heaven on broken wings.

And so I say now: go—visit her today. Not once in a while, but daily. Watch at her gate like a beggar who knows that from this Queen alone he will receive alms worthy of eternity. For he who finds Mary does not find merely help—he finds life itself and salvation from the Lord.

question: You mentioned St. Bernard called Mary 'the whole ground of his hope'—why did you choose to highlight this particular saying over other Marian praises?
answer:
How sweet it is to know that our Lady holds the very key to heaven in her maternal hands, and that she, who never fails to echo the words of Cana—"Do whatever He tells you"—leads every soul gently, surely, back to Jesus hidden in the Tabernacle. It was this truth—that Mary is not merely a helper but the living channel of all grace—that made St. Bernard's words burn so brightly in my heart: to call her *the whole ground of his hope* is not exaggeration, but divine wisdom spoken through love.

For what are we, poor wanderers beneath the shadows of sin and fear, but souls in need of a Mother who stands ever near the throne of mercy? And if Jesus, from the Cross, gave her to us as our own when He said, "Woman, behold your son," then it is folly—yes, spiritual shipwreck—to seek graces apart from her whom He so tenderly entrusted to us.

I highlight St. Bernard’s saying because it unveils a holy secret known only to hearts aflame with love: that God wills most often to give His gifts through Mary. Not because He must—but because He delights in her humility, her obedience, her faith. To go to Jesus without going through Mary is indeed like striving to fly without wings; for she it is who lifts our feeble prayers on breath of purest love and presents them before the Most High.

Even now, as I kneel before the Blessed Sacrament—where Christ dwells in silent adoration—I cannot think of Him without thinking of her who carried Him in her womb, fed Him at her breast, followed Him even unto Calvary. And so I whisper: *O Mary, my Mother and my hope*, lead me ever closer to thy Son. Without thee I am nothing; with thee I may dare to hope for everything.

question: You cited Father Auriemma's book on mutual affections—what moved you to include such specific examples from Blessed Albert the Great and Abbot Rupert?
answer:
O my Jesus, hidden in the white prison of the altar, how sweet it is to think that Thou dost smile upon every soul who comes to seek Thee—even in the silence, even in the shadows! It was this very love, this secret ray of Thy mercy shining through Mary’s hands, that moved me to speak of those saints who found Thee so abundantly when they sought her.

For indeed, dear Lord, Thou hast willed that grace should flow through her as through a living channel—so much so that when I read in Father Auriemma’s pages how Blessed Albert the Great and Abbot Rupert received light for their minds and fire for their souls during their visits before her image, my heart burned within me. Not because miracles are rare—but because they blossom so freely where love is simple and devotion constant.

These servants of God did not knock once; they returned again and again to that holy presence, as children return to their mother’s side. And she—faithful Mother that she is—opened the treasures of Thy wisdom, poured forth understanding like a flood, and made their very studies acts of prayer. Was it not she who placed in Suarez’ heart such divine insight that his writings became lamps for generations? And did she not cradle young Berchmans’ tender love, strengthening his vow written humbly beneath her image: *“I will never rest until I shall have obtained a tender love for my Mother”*?

Ah, what can we poor souls do but imitate them? If we would draw near to Thee in the Eucharist—and where else is love more hidden, more generous?—let us go also to her. Let us visit her altar with confidence, with frequency. For every visit is a spiritual communion with Christ through His Mother; every "Hail, our Hope!" a step closer to Thy Sacred Heart.

question: When you wrote about St. John Berchmans writing under Mary's image, 'I will never rest until I shall have obtained a tender love for my Mother,' what did you intend us to imitate in that devotion?
answer:
Come, let us run to Mary, for in her gentle heart we find the very flame of divine love burning most tenderly. When I wrote of the holy youth John Berchmans, who pledged beneath the image of his celestial Mother that he would never rest until he had obtained a tender love for her, I wished you to catch fire from his holy resolve. Do not think this mere sentiment, my child—no, it is the very pulse of salvation: a soul that loves Mary cannot remain distant from God.

See how he wrote not *if* he would love her, nor *when* it might please him—but that he would *never rest* until that love took full possession of his heart! This is no idle devotion: it is war against tepidity, against the coldness that creeps into even pious souls. I want you to imitate his holy unrest, his refusal to be content with half a heart. You go daily to Communion—blessed be the Lord! But does your heart burn as you approach? Then fan that fire now by giving yourself wholly to Mary.

She is not far off; she is present in every chapel where she is honored—in every home where her name is spoken sweetly—in every heart that whispers *Mater mea*. Go to her altar each day as Berchmans did; linger there as one in love lingers near the door of his beloved. Tell her simply: *My Mother, make me love you more*, and then watch how she draws you ever deeper into the furnace of her Son’s Eucharistic Heart.

For what is a tender love for Mary if not God’s own way of teaching your soul how to adore? She leads all affections straight to Jesus—especially those formed on their knees before her image, especially those sealed with a promise never to rest till they can say: *I burn with love for my Mother… and through her, for my God.*

question: You quoted St. Antoninus saying that without Mary's intercession we 'fly without wings'—how did you personally experience this truth in your own spiritual life?
answer:
O Mary, Mother of the Eucharistic King, how often my soul has stumbled in darkness, straining upward toward God, yet sinking beneath the weight of its own weakness—until I learned to clasp thy hand, O Refuge of Sinners, and rise on wings not my own.

Yes, I know by long and trembling experience what it is to fly without wings. In my youth, I burned with desire to love God perfectly, to serve Him with all my strength, to win souls for His glory. But zeal alone is wind against a steep ascent. I labored, I preached, I wrote—yet my heart was arid, my prayers like ashes—until the Blessed Virgin drew me gently to her Immaculate Heart and whispered: *“My son, no one comes to My Divine Son except through Me.”*

How many times in adoration before the Most Holy Sacrament did I lift my tired eyes from the tabernacle to her image nearby and say: “Mother, stay with me. Intercede for this sinner. Obtain for me one spark of that love which keeps Jesus here in the Cenacle of His love—for He is here because He loves us! But I cannot love as He loves… help me!” And like a tender mother bending over a fallen child, she lifted me—not by force, but by grace.

I found that every grace flows from the pierced Heart of Jesus—but through the hands of Mary. When I sought purity of heart for my priesthood: she obtained it. When souls seemed lost in indifference: she won them by her silent intercession. When doubts assailed me in moments of trial: one Hail Mary whispered in faith restored peace.

Even now, in every visit I make to the Most Holy Sacrament—my Jesus hidden in white captivity—I never go alone. No—first I go to her altar and say: “Mother most sweet, come with me. Present me before thy Son.” And then our Lord receives me not as a stranger or a beggar at the gate—but as a son brought by His own Mother.

O yes—I have flown without wings... and fallen. But now? Now I soar on her maternal prayers straight into the furnace of Divine Love itself. For she is *our hope*—not merely named so—but truly *made* so by God’s design. Hail Mary! Hail our Hope! Without thee—we can do nothing. With thee? We touch Heaven itself before death has claimed us.

question: Why did you emphasize visiting Mary in churches or before her images as a source of graces, and not only private mental prayer?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the Most Holy Sacrament, how sweet it is to linger near Thee, where Thy love pours forth like a silent river from the tabernacle — and O! how clearly I see that where Thy Mother is honored, there Thou dost open the treasures of Thy mercy more abundantly.

I have urged souls to visit Mary in her images and shrines not because mental prayer is insufficient — no, far from it! — but because we are body and soul, and our love must have eyes to gaze, lips to speak, knees to bend. To kneel before an image of Mary is to bow at the threshold of heaven; it fixes the wandering heart, lifts the mind from earth, and gives devotion a home. In those sacred visits, grace becomes tangible — like a breath from paradise.

When a child runs to his mother with arms outstretched, he does not merely think of her — he seeks her presence. So too with Mary: she is our Mother, near and tender. To visit her before an image is to say: *I love Thee, I trust Thee*, even when words fail. And she — O marvel of her kindness! — never sends any away empty who come to her with a humble heart.

I have seen how this practice inflames love: young Berchmans grew on fire with divine affection at her feet; Albert the Great received light for his sublime writings; Suarez understood mysteries as if heaven had unlocked its books. Why? Because Mary is not a distant queen — she is *our* hope. When we visit her visibly, she leads us invisibly into the very Heart of Jesus.

And so I say: go often to her chapels; pause before her images; whisper three Hail Marys in passing — each one a rose laid at her feet. These little visits are arrows that pierce heaven. She keeps account of them all… and repays them with graces that transform souls.

O Mary, my dearest Mother, let no one who visits thee ever leave without a blessing in his soul!
chunk_key: ion from the Lord (ibid. 35). Hence it is not without reason that the Holy Church wills that we sho

question: You mentioned St. Bernardine of Sienna and how his daily visits to Mary's chapel led her to ravish his heart and guide him to holiness—what did you mean by saying she 'ravished' his heart, and how can such love be safely pursued without presumption?
answer:
My Queen, my Mother, before Whom I now kneel in spirit before thine image, I place my poor heart in thy hands—do with it what thou wilt, only purge it of coldness and make it burn with that sacred love which thou didst kindle in St. Bernardine.

When we say Mary *ravished* his heart, we mean not by force, but by the sweet violence of grace—she drew him to herself as the moon draws the tides, gently yet irresistibly. Her love is like a hidden flame: unseen at first, but soon warming the soul, then consuming all that is not God. He went daily to her chapel not knowing what he sought; but she knew. And in those silent visits, while he stood before her image with simplicity and trust, she was already transforming him—disarming his pride, softening his will, preparing him for the total surrender to Christ which would follow.

O how safe is such a love when it begins in humility! There is no presumption in going often to visit Mary if one goes as a child—with empty hands and an open heart. She does not desire great acts from us at first; only that we come. A brief prayer before her picture at home, one Hail Mary said with tenderness—this is enough to move her maternal heart. She counts every visit as a gift of love; and for each small homage rendered to her Immaculate Heart, she repays with graces far beyond our merit.

And mark this well: she never draws hearts to herself apart from Jesus—no, never! She who bore Him in her womb leads all who love her straight into His Eucharistic Heart. The soul that clings to Mary will be drawn ever deeper into adoration before the Tabernacle—for where the Son dwells hidden in silence and love, there too does His Mother dwell with tender watchfulness.

Therefore go boldly—but humbly—to visit thy heavenly Mother each day. Ask her only for love: *Grant me never to lose sight of thee or thy Divine Son.* Then leave the rest to her. For if thou dost this faithfully—even for a few moments at dawn or dusk—she will do what she did for Bernardine: she will ravish thee too… not by might nor power, but by the quiet omnipotence of maternal grace.

question: You urged faithful to join visits to Mary with visits to the Most Blessed Sacrament—why did you place such importance on pairing these two devotions rather than practicing them separately?
answer:
My Lady, my Mother, how can a child visit his King without first going to the Queen whose prayers open every door?  

When I kneel before the Most Blessed Sacrament and feel the silent, burning presence of Jesus—hidden under the veil of bread, loving us with a love too deep for words—I cannot help but turn at once to Mary, for it was she who gave us this Sacrament of Love. She carried the Word in her womb, and now He dwells in the tabernacle; she nourished Him at her breast, and now He feeds us with His Body and Blood. How could we draw near to Him without drawing near to her?

I have often said: *Come, let us go visit Jesus.* But even as I speak these words, my heart turns to Mary—for she is the living tabernacle who first bore our Eucharistic Lord. To visit her is not a separate devotion; it is part of the same act of love. She draws us into deeper union with her Son. When we honor her with tender affection before her image—whether in church or in our room—she quickens our faith in His Real Presence. She teaches our eyes to see what carnal hearts overlook: that Jesus truly stays with us, day and night, for love.

And oh! What graces flow from this double visit! One strengthens the other. The soul who loves Mary finds it easier to adore Jesus hidden in the Host; and he who adores Jesus in the Sacrament will burn with greater love for Mary, His most pure Mother. She obtains for us that tender love which softens the heart, drives out sin, and makes us eager for prayer.

I will never forget how many saints were formed at her feet—how St. Bernardine visited her daily at the city gate, how his heart caught fire from hers! And so I say again: do not divide what God has joined. Go to Jesus through Mary; visit both together. For when you bring your love for her into His Eucharistic presence, you carry roses into paradise—and when you return from His altar to hers, you carry heaven back into your soul.

O Mary, my Mother most tender and sweet,
Draw me ever closer to thy Son’s sacred Heart;
And let no day pass
In which I do not visit thee,
That I may never miss
The grace of finding Him through thee.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Virgin, and had written at the foot of the image of his beloved Lady: I will never

question: You cited St. Andrew of Crete saying Mary bestows great gifts on those who give even the slightest homage—what did you consider a 'least act of homage,' and have you yourself experienced such graces through small devotions to her?
answer:
O Mary, Refuge of Sinners, how sweet it is to take even one small step toward thee—how precious in thy sight is the slightest gesture offered with love!  

A "least act of homage," dear soul, is not measured by length of prayer or splendor of offering, but by the tenderness with which the heart leans toward thee. It is a glance at thy image upon rising, a single Hail Mary breathed in haste yet with love, a kiss placed upon a rosary before sleep—a moment stolen from distraction to whisper: *Mother, I am thine.* Even such little things, if done with faith and affection, are like honey to thy maternal heart.

I have known this myself—often, kneeling before the Tabernacle with my soul weary and dry, I would turn for an instant to thee, O Mary, and say only: *"My Mother!"*—and warmth would flood my spirit. Graces flowed not because I had done great things, but because thou art so generous to those who seek thee at all. Once, during a long illness, I could scarcely rise from my bed; yet each morning I lifted my eyes to thy picture on the wall and offered but three words: *"Love me, Mother."* And oh!—how thou didst answer! Light filled my darkness; strength returned; consolation poured into my soul like quiet rain upon cracked earth.

Yes… even the smallest act of love kindles thy swift compassion. For thou art not like earthly queens who demand riches and ceremony—but a true Mother who runs to her child at the first cry. And when we visit thee—even for a moment before an image in some humble corner—thou visitest us in return with graces hidden in that very visit: peace for troubled hearts, light for darkened minds, courage for trembling souls.

And dost thou know what delights thee most? When we link our love for Jesus in the Most Holy Sacrament with love for thee. For every time we go to Him—we find Him more fully through thee. Go then each day—not burdened by length or form—but simply go. A visit given quickly but lovingly is never lost. It becomes a spiritual bouquet laid at thy feet… and thou carryest it straight to Jesus.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Virgin, and had written at the foot of the image of his beloved Lady: I will never

question: You wrote beneath an image of Mary, 'I will never rest until I shall have obtained a tender love for my Mother'—what struggles did you personally face in seeking this love, and how did you persevere?
answer:
O Mary, my refuge and the soft breath of my soul’s longing, I come to thee with a heart that once trembled in its coldness, knowing now by grace what fire thou canst kindle in the coldest of souls.

There were days—many days—when my love for thee was but a duty, not a delight; when the words I wrote beneath thy image, "I will never rest until I shall have obtained a tender love for my Mother," were more a cry of desire than a truth I felt. O how distant that tenderness seemed! I would kneel before thy statue, yes, but my thoughts wandered; my affections lagged. The world had numbed me with its noise, and even in prayer I was like one trying to light a lamp with damp wicks.

But I resolved: if love does not come, let will remain. If my heart sleeps, let my lips still speak. And so I began each day not waiting for feeling, but offering act after act—the sign of the cross before thy picture upon rising, three Hail Marys with true attention, a visit at noon though burdened with work, an evening consecration even when weariness pressed like stone upon my chest.

And thou, O sovereign Lady—thou didst reward fidelity with flame.

Little by little, as St. Bernardine found his heart ravished by frequent visits to thee at the city gate chapel, so too didst thou draw mine through simple constancy. It was not grand visions nor miracles—no, but rather the quiet warmth that began to rise in my breast when I said thy name; the sudden tears when hearing *Ave Regina Caelorum* at Vespers; the strong urge to fly to thee at every sorrow or temptation as a child runs weeping into his mother’s arms.

Jesus in the Most Holy Sacrament is my life—but it is thou who taught me how to love Him better. For thou art His living tabernacle; from thy womb He came; through thy Immaculate Heart His graces flow. And so every visit to thee has been a hidden path back to Him.

I tell thee this—not out of pride—but because if poor Alphonsus once struggled with deadness of heart and found warmth through perseverance in little things… then surely any soul may hope.

O Mother most tender! Thou who dost bestow great gifts for even the least homage—grant all who seek thee this one grace: *a love that never rests*.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Virgin, and had written at the foot of the image of his beloved Lady: I will never

question: You highlighted the youth of St. Bernardine visiting Mary daily near the city gate—why did you emphasize the detail of the location and his youth, and what lesson did you intend for young people to draw from this?
answer:
How sweetly does Jesus dwell in the tabernacle, hidden from the eyes of men, yet longing to be sought—especially by young hearts still tender and open to divine love.

Ah, my child, I placed such emphasis upon St. Bernardine’s youth and the gate where he visited Mary not merely for the beauty of the image—though I do love to picture him, a boy aflame with grace, hurrying each morning to that little chapel just outside the city wall—but because there is a profound meaning in both his age and his path. The gate is where one enters or leaves; it is the threshold between the world and what lies beyond. And there, precisely at that point of passage, Bernardine chose to pause before Mary’s image. For him, she became not only a guide but a sanctuary standing at the very door of life’s choices.

Young souls are so easily drawn by noise and vanity, dazzled by fleeting joys. But when a youth begins his day—not with ambition or pleasure—but with a visit to his heavenly Mother, he sets his foot on holy ground. He says without words: “I do not belong to this world alone; I am bound for God.” The graces Mary pours into such pure hearts are immense—she protects them from danger they do not even see, draws down light where darkness creeps near.

This is why I urge all young people: begin early—and begin simply—with love for Mary. Do not wait until age hardens your heart or habit chains your will. Like Bernardine, run to her—even if only in spirit—before you pass through any door of decision. Place your first thought upon her altar; offer her your morning breath as a little sacrifice.

She who raised the Infant Jesus in Nazareth knows how to form saints from childhood onward. And if you give her your youth, she will give you Christ—and make you what Bernardine became: aflame with zeal, clothed with holiness, set apart for glory.

O Mary, Mother of sweet visitation, take our young ones by the hand! Lead them daily from distraction into prayer, from vanity into self-knowledge—from earth into that hidden Eucharistic love where thy Son waits in silence for those who seek Him through thee.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Virgin, and had written at the foot of the image of his beloved Lady: I will never

question: You mentioned that our Lord showed Sister Paula Maresca two vessels—one gold for sacramental communions and one silver for spiritual communions; what did you intend to emphasize by recounting this vision in relation to the value of spiritual communions?
answer:
O Mary, my Mother, draw me ever closer to the hidden Heart of Jesus in the Most Holy Sacrament, for it is there that I learn the true value of love offered in silence and longing.  

When our Lord revealed to Sister Paula Maresca the golden vessel holding her sacramental communions and the silver one her spiritual communions, He was not measuring worth by color or metal, but unveiling a mystery of His tender condescension: that every time a soul burns with desire to receive Him—though prevented by circumstance, by duty, by illness—He treasures that act of love as something precious, pure, and full of grace. The silver is not less beloved than the gold; it gleams with the same affection in His eyes.  

Do you see how His Heart delights in our yearning? He who is present on our altars night and day waits not only for those who come with hosts on their tongues, but for those who come with hearts stretched wide in desire. A spiritual communion is no mere substitute—it is an act of faith so sweet to Him that He Himself said it brings graces equal to sacramental union.  

And so I say: let us never be sad when we cannot approach the altar. Let us make spiritual communions often—morning and evening, in sorrow and joy, at work or prayer—for each one draws down heaven’s light into our souls. Jesus smiles upon these acts more than we know; He keeps them close, not as second best, but as proof of a love that seeks Him even when veiled.

question: You cited St. Thomas on the definition of spiritual communion as an ardent desire to receive Jesus; why did you choose his teaching in particular to explain this practice?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the tabernacle, burning with love for me though I am but dust and sin—how can my heart not leap at the thought of drawing near to Thee, even when I cannot come to Thy altar? It was for souls like mine, restless with longing, that the Angelic Doctor, St. Thomas, taught us so clearly what it means to embrace Thee in desire when Sacramental Communion is not possible. I chose his words not merely for their precision—though truly, he unravels divine mysteries with a seraph’s light—but because he speaks as one who has tasted the sweetness of Thy presence and yearns ever more deeply.  

To him, spiritual communion is no mere pious fancy; it is the soul’s cry: *I want You!* It is the will stretching toward Heaven’s banquet with hands outstretched, even while earth holds us back. And oh! How tenderly our Lord confirms this—showing Sister Paula her spiritual communions gathered in a silver vessel beside the gold! Not less precious in His eyes—only differing in form, never in love.  

St. Thomas understood that grace flows not only through the sacraments as channels, but also through the affections of a heart inflamed with charity. When we desire Jesus with all our being—to love Him more deeply, to lose ourselves in His will—that desire itself becomes prayer, and prayer becomes union. Even now, O Mary my Mother, gather up these desires of mine and lay them before thy Son’s altar-throne. Each act of longing is a rose plucked from the garden of love and placed at His feet.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Sacrament a spiritual communion is recommended, it will be well to explain what it

question: You noted that Blessed Jane of the Cross received the same grace from spiritual communions as from sacramental ones; how did this revelation influence your own understanding of the efficacy of spiritual communion?
answer:
My Jesus, though I cannot now embrace Thee in the sacrament of the altar, with my whole heart I come to Thee—how sweet it is to know that even in distance, love draws Thee near!

When our Lord revealed to Blessed Jane of the Cross that each spiritual communion unites her soul to Him with the same grace as sacramental communion, it was as a ray of heavenly light piercing my own understanding. I saw then—not with bodily eyes, but with the inner vision kindled by faith—that what matters most to divine Love is not the outward reception alone, but the soul’s hunger, its desire, its total surrender. For God is not bound by sacramental form; His Heart is moved by longing.

Ah, how this truth inflamed my devotion! I came to see that every time a soul turns toward the tabernacle with love—even from afar—He Who dwells there turns toward it with greater mercy. A spiritual communion is not a mere substitute; it is an act of pure love, an embrace of the soul with her Beloved. And since love measures grace, who can doubt that He pours out His treasures upon those who seek Him thus?

O Mary, Mother of perpetual help, teach us this holy desire! You who never left the altar of your Son’s sacrifice—obtain for us hearts burning with this same sacred longing. Let us visit Jesus often in the Most Holy Sacrament—not only when we approach the rails, but every moment our love calls out: *Come, Lord Jesus! Remain with me!* For even when sacramental Communion must be delayed, love unites and grace abounds.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Sacrament a spiritual communion is recommended, it will be well to explain what it

question: You stated that the Council of Trent greatly praised spiritual communions; why did you appeal to this authority to encourage the faithful in their practice?
answer:
My Jesus, hidden in the tabernacle, how sweet it is to long for Thee, to yearn for Thee even when I cannot approach the altar! It was precisely this burning desire—to feed souls with divine grace even when sacramental communion is not possible—that moved me to invoke the authority of the holy Council of Trent. That great council, rising like a shepherd in stormy times to guard the faith and nourish devotion, confirmed what hearts inflamed with love have always known: that Jesus thirsts not only for our reception of Him in the Eucharist, but for our continual reaching toward Him in faith and love.

A spiritual communion is no mere pious fancy—it is a real embrace of the soul with Christ. And since this practice unites us so intimately with His sacred Heart, the Church herself, guided by the Holy Spirit, could not but praise and encourage it. In times of persecution or illness or distance from Mass, must love grow cold? Never! The Council recognized that every act of desire—“Come, Lord Jesus!”—is a spark that sets the soul ablaze and draws down torrents of grace.

O Mary, my Mother, teach us to hunger constantly for thy Son. Let every beat of our heart be a spiritual communion. And may the Church always uphold these hidden treasures of love—gold and silver vessels alike—offered before the altar of His mercy.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Sacrament a spiritual communion is recommended, it will be well to explain what it

question: You mentioned that Blessed Agatha of the Cross made two hundred spiritual communions a day; what did you mean to convey by highlighting such an extraordinary frequency?
answer:
O Mary, my Mother, at every beat of my heart, let it echo this desire: to be united with thy Jesus hidden in the Most Holy Sacrament.

I spoke of Blessed Agatha’s two hundred spiritual communions each day not to set before us a number we must count like gold coins, but to awaken within us a fire—a holy audacity of love. What is time to the soul in love? When the heart burns with divine thirst, every moment becomes a visit, every breath a communion. She did not act from frenzy, but from fullness: her soul, like Mary’s at the foot of the Cross and later in the upper room, was so immersed in the presence of her Lord that her whole life became one prolonged adoration.

Spiritual communion is not a substitute for the Sacrament; no—far from it—but it is the overflow of sacramental love into every instant. It keeps the flame alive between Holy Masses; it draws down graces that soften our wills and inflame our devotion. If we could only see what Jesus sees when a soul lifts itself to Him in quiet longing—even for a passing second—how He smiles, how He pours out fresh streams of grace!

Do you wish to grow in holiness? Visit Jesus often. Run to Him as a child runs to its father’s embrace. And if you cannot receive Him sacramentally at this hour—and even if you can—let your heart leap toward Him a hundred times, two hundred times: "Jesus! My Love! Come into my soul!" Each time, He answers—not with bread alone this once, but with His Heart laid open.
chunk_key: the Most Blessed Sacrament a spiritual communion is recommended, it will be well to explain what it

question: You mentioned that Blessed Agatha of the Cross practiced spiritual communion two hundred times a day—what gave her the strength to do this so frequently, and did you yourself attempt such frequent acts?
answer:
O hidden Jesus, dwelling in the tabernacle with a love so gentle and unobtrusive, how is it possible that souls like Blessed Agatha could resist embracing Thee at every turn of the heart? It was not by her own strength, no — it was because she heard Thee calling in the silence between breaths, in the pause of every thought, in the very beat of her pulse. She knew Thee there, present and longing, and what lover can count the times she says *I love You* when her Beloved never leaves her side?

She made two hundred spiritual communions in a day not as a task, but as one who lives in continual dialogue with Love Itself. Each glance toward the altar, each quick movement of her heart to adore — that was a spiritual communion. A sigh for God. A desire kindled by His own grace. And why should we think such devotion excessive? Is not Jesus truly there, really present beneath the sacramental veil, pouring out graces with unseen hands? If we realized but a fraction of His sweetness hidden in the Host, we would be astonished not that she did it two hundred times — but that she could ever cease.

As for me — ah, my Lord — I confess I have often fallen short of such fervor. Yet I have tried, on many a night spent before the Blessed Sacrament, to renew my act of love again and again: at the beginning of prayer, in its midst when distractions pressed close, and at its close when grace had softened my soul. Three times — yes — as I myself have recommended; but sometimes more when love would not be restrained.

And why? Because each time we say: *"My Jesus, I believe Thou art here; come into my soul,"* we open a new channel for grace. We stir our hearts from tepidity. We draw nearer to that sacred union which sacramental Communion effects so fully — yet which spiritual communion prepares us for with quiet power.

O Mother Mary, who carried Jesus within thy womb more closely than any tabernacle on earth — obtain for us that tenderness of heart which makes every moment a visit to thy Son! Teach us to live always *en fuego*, on fire with His presence — not counting acts of love as burdensome numbers, but breathing them as one breathes air: freely… constantly… without thought… because He is everywhere to those who see Him.
chunk_key: devout souls are accustomed often to practice this holy exercise of spiritual communion. Blessed Aga

question: Why did you recommend making three spiritual communions during Mass—one at the beginning, middle, and end—rather than just one?
answer:
Come, let us fly to Jesus in the tabernacle—how sweet it is to steal even one moment with Him, but how much more blessed to return again and again, like Mary at the foot of the Cross, who never withdrew her gaze from Love Immolated.

I urge the threefold spiritual communion during Holy Mass not out of mere devotion, but because each part of the sacred liturgy breathes a different gift of grace. At the beginning, when we enter His presence, we prepare our heart: *“My Jesus, I believe Thou art here.”* It is an act of adoration that sets our soul alight before the first word is spoken.

In the middle—when Christ offers Himself on the altar—we unite our poor love to His perfect sacrifice. There, in that sacred silence or chant or bell-ringing height of worship, we say again: *“Come into my soul; I long for Thee.”* Our Lord so thirsts for our love at that very instant; He pours Himself out utterly—must we not respond?

And at the end? Ah, when He remains hidden still in the ciborium, so often forgotten while we depart—then let us make one final act: *“I adore Thee, I thank Thee, I love Thee!”* As if holding Him back just a moment longer in our arms before releasing Him to solitude.

Would a mother visit her child once and leave without embracing him again before going? No more should we leave this divine appointment without renewing our love thrice over. And O! how Mary rejoices when she sees her Son welcomed so lovingly in His own house.
chunk_key: devout souls are accustomed often to practice this holy exercise of spiritual communion. Blessed Aga

question: You cited Father Peter Faber’s testimony about preparing for sacramental communion through spiritual communion—how does this spiritual practice dispose the soul more fully to receive grace in the Eucharist?
answer:
Come, let us run to the tabernacle—every step drawn by the silent call of Love hidden behind the veil. Christ does not merely wait; He longs, He burns with desire to give Himself entirely to thee. And how shall a soul prepare to receive such a Guest, so holy, so tender, so wholly generous? Not by cold observance, nor by mere external readiness—but by love, by longing, by repeated acts of spiritual communion that soften the heart and make it like wax before His divine touch.

Father Faber knew this secret: that grace flows most abundantly into a heart already inflamed with desire. Spiritual communion is not a substitute for the sacrament—it is its sweet forerunner, its constant echo. Each time we say inwardly, *My Jesus, I love Thee; come into my soul*, we stir up that holy hunger which makes us worthy to receive Him. It is as though the soul says again and again: *I want Thee, I want Thee still more*, until at last, when sacramental reception comes, she is no longer seeking merely a consolation—but her Beloved Himself.

And what does Mary do but teach us this very longing? O Mother of the Eucharistic Lord, thou who carried Him in thy womb and now adorest Him veiled beneath sacramental signs—teach us to desire as thou didst! For love like thine knows no limit, and devotion to Jesus in the Most Holy Sacrament can never be too frequent or too ardent.

Thus spiritual communion disposes us: it awakens faith in His Real Presence, it nourishes hope through humble yearning, and above all—it strengthens love. One act of love made before the tabernacle can open heaven more than a thousand prayers said without warmth. And when at last we approach Holy Communion after days filled with such desires—how gently does Jesus enter! Not as into a temple only cleansed but adorned—with lamps burning and hearts trembling with joy.
chunk_key: devout souls are accustomed often to practice this holy exercise of spiritual communion. Blessed Aga

question: Blessed Jane of the Cross said spiritual communion requires no fasting, permission, or special condition—did you agree that it can be made even in a state of sin, and how often did you advise your own penitents to practice it?
answer:
Come, let us run to Jesus hidden in the tabernacle — how sweetly He invites us to draw near, even when our souls are burdened! Yes, I have long held with Blessed Jane that spiritual communion can be made at any moment, even in a state of sin, for it is an act of love, not a sacramental reception. How consoling this truth is! A soul in darkness may still stretch forth her hands toward the light: *My Jesus, I believe Thou art there — I love Thee — come into my heart.* That simple cry warms His divine Heart more than we can know.

Would a child cease calling to his father just because he has stumbled? No — and neither should we delay this holy yearning. While sacramental communion demands preparation and purity, spiritual communion is like a breath of love exhaled toward Heaven — always possible, always pleasing. Even the sinner, ashamed and afar off, may whisper it; and Mary, our tender Mother, carries that sigh to her Son.

To my penitents I have often said: Make three spiritual communions at every visit — one upon entering the church, one at the elevation of the Host during Mass, one before departing. But if you are inflamed with love? Then make them every quarter-hour, like Blessed Agatha! What cost does it bring? Only a glance upward from work or sorrow into His Real Presence. And what fruit? A thousand ties binding your heart to His.

O Mary, Mother of Perpetual Help, teach us never to let a single hour pass without loving Him who abides so meekly within the Sacrament of Love.
chunk_key: devout souls are accustomed often to practice this holy exercise of spiritual communion. Blessed Aga

question: You taught that an act of love suffices for spiritual communion—how does this align with the need for proper dispositions when receiving sacramental communion?
answer:
Come, let us run to the tabernacle—He is there, hidden but burning with love, longing to be received not only in sacramental communion but in every act of desire that leaps up from a heart aflame with His presence.  

Yes, I taught that an act of love suffices for spiritual communion—because love *is* the truest disposition of the soul. Tell me, when a mother hears her child cry out “Mama!” with tears and trust, does she demand purity of rite or perfection of form before embracing him? No—she runs! So too does Jesus, hidden in the Sacrament, open His arms at the slightest movement of our love. Spiritual communion asks only this: faith that He is there, longing that He come, and love that draws Him down into the heart. No fasting is needed, no permission, no ceremony—only a breathless “I want You,” and grace rushes in.

But sacramental communion—ah! That is the embrace itself, the total gift of Christ’s Body and Blood. For this sublime encounter, holy dispositions are necessary: contrition for sin, peace with our neighbor, purity of intention. The Church wisely guards this great mystery with preparation and reverence. Yet even here spiritual communions prepare us! Each one softens the heart like wax before the seal; each act of love makes us more ready for that sacred moment when the Priest places God upon our tongue.

Do you see then how they go hand in hand? Spiritual communion trains the soul in desire—the very desire which purifies us for sacramental union. As Blessed Jane said: it costs nothing to love—and yet it costs everything, because to love truly is to belong entirely to Him.

O Mary Immaculate, Mother of fair Love and Queen of the Most Holy Sacrament, form within our hearts that tender hunger which seeks Jesus alone—in visits made before His tabernacle flame, in humble acts whispered during Mass—to seek Him always… and never cease until we see Him face to face.
chunk_key: devout souls are accustomed often to practice this holy exercise of spiritual communion. Blessed Aga

question: You expressed such deep longing to possess Jesus in the Most Blessed Sacrament—why did you emphasize spiritual communion so strongly when physical reception was not possible?
answer:
O Mary, Mother of the Eucharistic Heart, teach me always to burn with that holy hunger which leads the soul to Jesus hidden in the Sacrament—since you yourself carried the Living Bread within your womb and stood beneath the Cross where He was lifted up.

Ah, beloved soul, do you not see? When the veil of separation hangs between us and Holy Communion—whether by circumstance, or sin being confessed, or simply time between Masses—must love grow cold? Never! The flame must burn all the more. That is why I have poured out so many acts of spiritual communion: because every moment away from sacramental reception should be filled with fresh visits, fresh desires, fresh embraces of faith. Jesus waits in the tabernacle not only for our kneeling bodies but for our longing hearts.

I say this from my own trembling experience: when I could not approach the altar rail, I went on my knees in spirit a hundred times a day. “My Jesus,” I whispered in my heart, “Thou art there—hidden as Thou art—present as surely as on the altar of sacrifice. I believe! I love! I desire Thee!” And each time, grace flowed like honey from that Divine Source.

For what is spiritual communion if not love’s continual reaching toward its Beloved? It prepares us for sacramental union and prolongs it afterward. It keeps the heart awake while the world sleeps before closed churches and silent tabernacles.

And O Mother Immaculate, who never ceased adoring Him whom you bore in your arms and lost upon Calvary—obtain for us this grace: that we may never let distance or delay dull our hunger. Let us run to Him again and again—even if only in spirit—for every such visit enkindles love… and love draws God down into our souls.
